So moving on to concepts. Evals really are critical to building successful AI products. Unlike traditional software engineering where you write, pass, fail unit tests and get consistent results. LLMs, as you know, behave very differently. Outputs are non-deterministic. You might run the same prompt twice and get slightly different answers each time. And even when the outputs are consistent, they're often subjective. What good looks like depends on context tone, accuracy, task alignment, and other factors. And that's really why we need evals. Evals help us measure model behavior. Crucially, establishing a baseline so that when we make changes, we can validate whether they're improving things. So whether you're tweaking a prompt, switching out a model, or changing how data is retrieved or perhaps even your user interaction has changed over time. Evals are what tell you if it's working and if it's working well. So this isn't a nice to have. Evals aren't optional, or at least they shouldn't be. If you're building real world production level AI applications, they're foundational to building and shipping reliable AI products with confidence. And just like in software engineering, test driven development helped engineers move faster with fewer regressions. Eval driven development is what unlocks the same kind of iteration. And confidence when you are building with AI and getting started is much easier than you think. You don't need to be an ML engineer and you don't need to have a massive, dedicated infra setup. You don't even need a huge data set to begin with. But what you do need is a willingness to define what good looks like for your product, and a handful of representative examples to test against. So we've talked about why evals matter, but what exactly is an eval? Really simply, evals are a way to test how well a language model performs on a specific task, whether that's extracting information, generating summaries, classifying inputs, or something else entirely. They help us measure things like accuracy, consistency, and progress over time. So instead of relying on anecdotes and gut feels evals, give us a repeatable structured way to track changes and compare different models, prompt approaches, techniques. Um, over time, each eval is built from four key ingredients. So first, you of course need a data set and that includes your input prompts and then some ground truth correct answers. So labels, um, of those Im input prompts. And then we need a model, which is the LLM that we're testing. We'd need a grader, which can be rule-based, like exact match, and we'll talk a little bit more about this or model graded using natural language feedback. And then some metrics. So these can be like pass fail rates, um, or more detailed ones, uh, that you often find in machine learning, uh, like F1 rouge blue, and all of these others as well. So this setup can help us automate what would otherwise be incredibly tedious and allows us to scale. You can run dozens or hundreds of tests, track regressions and improve your system iteratively. That's what evals are bringing to the table here. So this is, um, a way to consider your eval maturity and where you're at currently. So we've got a full spectrum here. At level one. We have basic, and on the other side, on the right hand side, we have level four flywheel. So at the basic level, this is really where you're not doing structured evals at all. Most decisions are made by gut feel, you know, if a prompt looks right or a completion feels good, but the risk of this is that you can't reliably track or catch regressions. Every prompt change feels quite scary because you don't know what might break it. And then when you move up a step into manual evals, what you're looking at is you've got some process in place. So maybe subject matter experts are reviewing outputs with spreadsheets or checklists, and this can be a good start, but it can be painful. Reviews are slow, it's expensive, and it's quite inconsistent as well, because depending on who is, um, evaluating, um, the criteria can drift between reviewers and it's better than nothing, but it's still quite a reactive, uh, way to approach things and quite unsustainable. Then we have automated evals, which is really where things are starting to click. This is where you've defined evals around real tasks. You version them and you can run them automatically across different models and prompt variants. And it means that you can start iterating with more confidence. You might still find blind spots, so coverage might be incomplete, some tasks might still require manual review, but this is the tipping point towards true maturity when it comes to developing your evals. And then finally, we have this flywheel effect, which is level four, and this is where evals become part of your core product lifecycle. It's where they run continuously in CICD against real world live data, um, based on traces. Um, and it allows you to surface issues before users ever see them. And no manual checks at this stage are needed to deploy because you've got high trust in your evals and feedback from production improves your prompts and data sets automatically. So evals are really part of your core infrastructure, and this is the point where we really want to get to. Now, most teams today sit about level two and they're, you know, experimenting with level three and then moving up to, uh, automated. Um, but the goal here really isn't perfection. It's progress. So no matter where you are, even if you aren't doing evals today, even chucking, um, a few examples in a spreadsheet and reviewing them, it's better than not doing it at all. So moving up even one step unlocks, you know, faster iteration and gives you more confidence and assurance, um, of your models, um, and of your production, uh, systems. And what we see as well is evals typically fall into three categories, depending on who or what is doing the grading. So at the top level, you have deterministic checks. These are rule-based checks, so you might think exact matches. Um, you might be doing some re rejects, uh, to pull out, you know, um, exact, uh, matches, uh, for example, um, or, or other kind of patterns from your data, um, or structured formats like JSON. You'll use this for tasks with clear verifiable outputs like classification, field extraction, timestamp, matching schema conformance, and others as well. And these are fast reproducible and easy to debug, but they can't handle nuance or fuzzy correctness. And that's where you would want to consider model graded evals. And here is where LLMs are the graders. You give them a rubric and you ask it to assess things like correctness, helpfulness or reason reasoning quality. This is useful for subjective or open-ended tasks like summarization, retrieval, relevance, you know, multiterm reasoning. And it scales a lot better than humans, um, and allows for more comprehensive, um, evals, but it needs a capable model and it needs a carefully written grading prompt as well. Finally, we have human graded evals, uh, which, um, as, as they sound like, uh, is where humans review model outputs using scoring guides or freeform judgment. And they typically tend to be the most expensive, but they're also the most trusted. It's necessary when quality, um, needs to, needs to be, you know, uh, really high. So you validate model graded or deterministic evals, um, against human labeled, uh, ground truth. Now downstream as well. Um, so that can be a great thing to do. And then you have agent handoff where the agent decides to hand off to another agent, um, which is, you know, a common pattern in multi-agent systems. Um, and one scenario, uh, that you might want to consider is, is the output from one agent passed to, um, the other agent? And is that context carried through and does the next agent have enough context to continue? Was the task correctly scoped? Was it reframed? All of these kind of things. So you can treat evals like tests. Um, so every time you that you make a change to a prompt, a model, anything else, you can run your evals again, um, see how they perform. So include the prompt version, uh, include the model id, it's incredibly easy to lose track, um, you know, uh, of, uh, of, of your, um, of your runs. Uh, so, you know, make sure you have that, um, standard naming schema. Um, and then you can also run proposed changes against a snapshot of real production traffic as we've, um, talked about, um, previously as well post-deployment. Um, you can continue running your evals and stored completions, um, that you are capturing. Um, you can do it periodically or on demand, um, depending on your use case. Um, and, you know, doing this helps detect issues caused by upstream prompt changes. Again, like changes to model snapshots that can happen, uh, depending on the model that you are pinned to, um, or unexpected inputs as well. So this setup really does turn evals into a complete monitoring surface, like another way to monitor how your system, um, is, uh, acting in the real world. Um, it's not just, you know, theoretical and it's not just a gate before you go live into production. I'll leave you with this quote. Um, I love this because I think it is incredibly relevant, uh, to what we're aiming for and what we're doing with evals here is that you can't improve what you don't measure. You do not know if your system is getting better, if you are actually making improvements unless you are tracking it. Um, you don't know if you know, fine tuning, for example, um, is actually, um, impr like a valid thing to do, a valid technique if you are not, uh, measuring against a set of evals and you have a baseline and you can measure how much you know, these techniques, um, these different, um, approaches, um, you know, are, are actually, uh, are actually working.
